q: ___ is the branch of biology that seeks to understand the molecular basis of biological activity in and between cells.
The study of chemical, physical structures, and functions of biological macromolecules is known as ___.
Nucleic acids are the main ___ molecules of the cell which direct the process of protein synthesis.
The two main classes of nucleic acids are ___ and ribonucleic acid (RNA).
DNA is the ___ for life and constitutes the genetic material in all free-living organisms and most viruses.
RNA is the genetic material of certain viruses, but it is also found in all living cells, where it plays an important role in certain processes such as the ___.
DNA is a ___ structure consisting of two polynucleotide strands twine around each other.
Each polynucleotide strand is made up of many ___ molecules.
Each nucleotide in DNA is composed of a five-carbon deoxyribose sugar, a ___, and a nitrogen-containing base.
The nitrogenous bases in DNA that can be categorized into two different forms: purines and ___.
The purine bases are ___ and guanine (G).
The pyrimidine bases are ___ and thymine (T).
The purine bases have two ___ in their chemical structures.
The pyrimidine bases have a ___ in their chemical structure.
Each strand forms a backbone of alternating ___ and sugars.
The bases of each nucleotide are attached to each sugar and protrude ___ at regular intervals along each strand.
There is a constant ___ between the sugar-phosphate backbones.
The two strands of a DNA molecule are not identical but are, instead, ___ to each other.
This means that a purine molecule is always paired with a ___ molecule.
Adenine (A) always pairs with ___ (T) and guanine (G) always pairs with cytosine (C).
This pairing is called ___.
___ link each complementary base pair.
A and T are joined by ___ hydrogen bonds, while C and G with three hydrogen bonds.
The two strands of a DNA molecule are ___.
One strand lies in $5'$ to $3'$ direction and another strand in ___ direction.
The $5'$ and $3'$ come from the numbering of the carbons on the ___.
The phosphate group is on the ___ carbon, and the OH group is on the $3'$ carbon.
By convention, the sequence of a DNA strand is always written in the ___ direction.
RNA is a polymer of nucleotides and contains four nucleotides with the bases adenine (A), ___, cytosine (C), and guanine (G).
Unlike DNA, RNA is ___.
tRNA can fold back on itself, and complementary base pairing within the same molecule stabilizes the ___.
___ acts as the template for translation.
___ carries specific amino acid to mRNA at ribosome.
___ reads the codon on mRNA.
DNA is the ___ material responsible for passing genetic information from cell to cell and generation to generation.
In total, there are around ___ billion base pairs in the DNA of a typical mammalian cell.
DNA replication is the making of an exact copy of the ___.
The replication is made by a ___ method, which was predicted by Watson and Crick.
Replication is regarded as semiconservative because each of the resulting two molecules consists of ___ and one new strand.
Replication begins at special sites called ___.
___ enzymes unwind the double helix and unzip the two parental strands by breaking hydrogen bonds.
___ binding proteins act as scaffolding, holding the two strands apart.
___ lessens the tension on the tight helix.
At each end of the replication bubble is a ___, a Y-shaped region where the new strands of DNA are elongating.
DNA polymerase III cannot initiate the synthesis; the preexisting chain actually consists of RNA primer produced by an enzyme called ___.
DNA polymerase can only add complementary nucleotides to the free ___ end of the primer.
A new DNA strand can elongate only in the ___ direction.
The strand formed towards the replication fork in continuous and linear fashion is called the ___.
The other strand, the ___, forms in direction away from the replication fork, in a series of segments.
The segments of the lagging strand are called ___.
Each Okazaki fragment is about ___ nucleotides long.
Okazaki fragments are joined into one continuous strand by the enzyme ___.
DNA polymerase I carry out ___ repair, a kind of proofreading that corrects errors.
Damaged regions of DNA are excised out by ___.
To protect against the possible loss of genes at the end of the chromosomes, eukaryotes have special nonsense nucleotide sequences ($TAAGGG$) called ___.
Telomeres are created and controlled by the enzyme ___.
